headline: Large Energy User Grid Cost Shifting
short_summary: This bill aims to adjust how large energy users, such as data centers, contribute to grid infrastructure costs, potentially reducing the burden on residential ratepayers.
status: Last Action: Governor's Action Deadline 11:59 p.m., May 23, 2026 on 2026-04-22
supporters: Residential consumer advocacy groups, environmental organizations, local governments.
opponents: Data center industry, large industrial energy users, utility companies.
news_headline: Governor amends bills shifting data center costs; critics say weakened.
news_summary: Governor Spanberger proposed amendments to HB 1393, which aimed to shift grid infrastructure and capacity costs onto high-load energy users like data centers, potentially saving residential customers money. While some amendments were accepted, critics argue the governor's changes weakened the original intent of fully shifting costs.

headline: Data Center Water Consumption Reporting
short_summary: This bill mandates that data centers report their water usage, increasing transparency regarding their environmental footprint.
status: Last Action: Acts of Assembly Chapter text (CHAP0896) on 2026-04-13
supporters: Environmental groups, water conservation organizations, local communities.
news_headline: Virginia data center water consumption reporting bill enacted.
news_summary: SB 553, now enacted, requires developers of proposed industrial facilities, including data centers, to provide annual water consumption projections as part of rezoning and special use permit applications. This measure increases transparency on water usage, giving localities more data to shape site selection in water-constrained areas.

headline: Data Center Siting and Noise Limits
short_summary: This bill establishes regulations for the location and noise levels of data centers, addressing local community concerns about their impact.
status: Last Action: Incorporated by Counties, Cities and Towns (HB153-Thomas) (Voice Vote) on 2026-01-23
supporters: Local residents, community associations, environmental groups concerned with quality of life.
opponents: Data center developers, real estate companies, economic development agencies.
news_headline: New Virginia bills target data center noise, siting.
news_summary: HB 153 establishes a new permit process for high energy use facilities, requiring site assessments that evaluate noise impacts on residences and schools within a 500-foot radius. This bill aims to mitigate negative impacts on residential areas and allows localities to consider additional environmental factors during zoning approval.
